steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage describes two things: (1) narrow- and intermediate-headed fish have increasing snout pressure fluctuations as vortex size grows, while wide-headed fish stay low; (2) the second team hypothesizes that higher pressure fluctuations help fish avoid obstructions. We need a finding that directly supports that link: high fluctuations = more avoidance (fewer bumps), low fluctuations = less avoidance (more bumps).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'the wide-headed Bagarius bumped into more than half of the obstructions': Reports a single wide-headed species (low fluctuations, many bumps) but gives no high-fluctuation comparison group. Without a contrast, this doesn't test the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice B — 'the intermediate-headed Mustelus bumped into obstructions just as often as the wide-headed Synodontis': If both bump equally, fluctuation level makes no difference — this contradicts, not supports, the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice C — 'the wide-headed Synodontis bumped into obstructions more often than the intermediate-headed Mustelus': Wide-headed = low fluctuations = more bumps; intermediate-headed = high fluctuations = fewer bumps. This is exactly the contrast the hypothesis predicts. ✓
  • Choice D — 'some specimens of the intermediate-headed rainbow trout bumped more often than others of the same species': Variation within a single species tells us nothing about whether fluctuation level drives avoidance. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
